title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
[MUSIC PLAYING] NATALIA MARKOBORODOVA:
Hi, I'm Natalia. And today, I'm going
to talk about how to build identity payments
and embedded widget solutions when third-party
cookies are restricted. Let's get started. Identity payments and
embedded widgets often rely on third-party cookies. Third-party cookies have been
on the web for a long time. And many developers use
them in their applications. However, third-party
cookies are also enabling cross-site
tracking and expose users to security issues, such as
cross-site request forgery. To protect users,
browsers have started blocking third-party
cookies by default or giving their users
the choice to block them. This means developers
should audit their solutions and update it if they rely
on third party cookies. This will help to avoid breakage
for users who don't have third-party cookies blocked. We have good news. With Privacy Sandbox
APIs, you don't have to choose between
privacy and functionality. Why would you want to
use Privacy Sandbox APIs. Most importantly,
Privacy Sandbox APIs offer robust and
enduring solutions. They can ensure your
site's functionality remains seamless, regardless of
third-party cookies availability Privacy Sandbox
APIs are designed to limit the information
that is shared about the user and reduce the scope of
potential user tracking. Solutions dependent
on third-party cookies may fail when
cookies are blocked. Let's take a look
at how to detect whether identity payments
or embedded widgets rely on third-party
cookies on your site. If you don't embed any content,
don't distribute or rely on cross-site widgets,
content, or scripts, and don't reuse the same session
cookie across multiple sites, there is a chance you don't
rely on third-party cookies. But today's web is composable. You probably don't implement
everything from scratch. And most external identity
and payment solutions rely on third-party cookies. So it never hurts
to double-check. The next step is to
ensure that all users get the same experience,
whether or not
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
You probably don't implement
everything from scratch. And most external identity
and payment solutions rely on third-party cookies. So it never hurts
to double-check. The next step is to
ensure that all users get the same experience,
whether or not third-party cookies
are available. The best way to do so is to
go through the user flows with third-party
cookies disabled. To disable third-party
cookies in Chrome. Go to DevTools, open
Privacy and security panel. Go to the Controls tab. And enable Temporarily
limit third-party cookies. Another way to disable cookies
is through Chrome flags. Navigate to Chrome flags
in your browser's URL bar. Enable the Test Third
Party Cookie flag. And you'll find
other useful flags that can help you to fine-tune
your browser configuration for testing purposes. Now that you're all
set up for testing, let's go through some
user flows that commonly rely on third-party cookies. Let's start with identity. Here is a checklist
for you to go through. Can the user create
a new account? Can they use a sign-in? Do personalized
sign-in buttons work? Does the sign-in status
persist when they navigate to other related domains? Can they use a sign-out? Do they remain signed out when
navigating through the site? Can the user recover their
password successfully? Remember to check these flows
for every third-party identity provider you integrate
with your solution. All right, identities checked. Let's move to another
essential component of the online
experience-- payments. Here is a checklist
to go through with third-party
cookies blocked. Can the user check out
with third-party providers, such as pay with
example provider? Can users add, update, and
delete payment methods? Do users see their saved payment
methods when making a purchase? Do personalized payment
buttons render as expected? Can the user complete purchases
even if the personalized button doesn't display the
preferred payment method? Does your payment fraud
detection work as expected? Do users have to go
through extra steps?
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
Do personalized payment
buttons render as expected? Can the user complete purchases
even if the personalized button doesn't display the
preferred payment method? Does your payment fraud
detection work as expected? Do users have to go
through extra steps? Can your software still
detect suspicious patterns? Every solution is unique. You may have an
embedded widget that relies on third-party cookies
outside of identity and payments use cases. It's always a good
idea to test your site. Here are some other
common examples to check. Can your user
access chat widgets? Can they resume the chat session
if they navigate to other pages and domains and back? What about social media? Can your users
comment, like, repost? Can your users access
content, for example, with an embedded player? Can they access premium content
if they have a subscription. Do data dashboard
widgets load correctly? Does the dashboard
display all the data users expect to see accordingly
with their saved preferences? Can users navigate
between different sections of the dashboard? Think of any other
embedded widgets-- maps, scheduling, contact forms. Do they all work as expected? Does your solution have another
creative embedded widget? Tell us in the comments. But what is actually
happening under the hood? Let's take a closer look
at the context pattern behind these examples. Let's go back to
the example flows we've discussed before, like
payment buttons or chat widgets. These flows are
often implemented as embedded widgets or scripts. It doesn't matter if the
widget or script is provided by a third-party company or
if it's your company that implements and uses the widget. If a widget is embedded on
a site different from where it is hosted, it is
considered third party. The cookies set
within a widget that are associated with
the widget's domain and that are embedded
on a different site are also third party. Of course, if the widget is
hosted on the same domain as the page that embeds it,
cookies set by the widget are first party.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
are associated with
the widget's domain and that are embedded
on a different site are also third party. Of course, if the widget is
hosted on the same domain as the page that embeds it,
cookies set by the widget are first party. A third-party script can also
set a first-party cookie. For example, if a script from
third-party.example is embedded on site.example, it can set
a cookie associated with site.example using
document.cookie property. This cookie will be considered
first party within site.example and won't be sent with network
requests to third-party.example. Now that we know what
third-party cookies are, let's take a look at what
happens if they are not available to the user. Imagine you have two sites-- site.example that embeds a
third-party widget or script and provider.example that
provides embeddable content, scripts, or widgets. When the user
visits site.example, they see its address
in the URL bar. It means that the user
visits site.example in a top-level context. Site.example embeds a widget
provided by provider.example. This widget can be
anything really-- for example, a payment
form, a user login, a fraud detection script,
or simply a weather widget. Imagine streaming.example
is a content provider. The user visits
streaming.example in the top-level context. When the user signs
in, streaming.example writes a cookie stored
in the user session. The signed-in user
has a subscription. So they have unlimited
access to premium content. Streaming.example
distributes a player widget, which other sites can embed. Now let's take a look
at a concrete example. Say a famous blogger uploads
a video on streaming.example. Only users with a
premium subscription can access the video. They also embed this video on
their personal blog.example site. Then, the user who's signed
in on streaming.example and has a premium subscription
is now visiting blog.example. To grant access to the user, the
streaming.example player widget needs to validate
their subscription. For that, the
streaming.example widget
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
Then, the user who's signed
in on streaming.example and has a premium subscription
is now visiting blog.example. To grant access to the user, the
streaming.example player widget needs to validate
their subscription. For that, the
streaming.example widget needs to read the
cookie that was set in the top-level context
when the user visited the streaming.example site. Now, here is what happens if
third-party cookies are not available. The widget is embedded
on blog.example, which is a different site
than streaming.example. This means that the cookie
set in the top-level context of streaming.example
is a third-party cookie to the widget. The widget cannot
access the cookie. And the user wouldn't be
able to access the video. This happens because the user is
currently visiting another site. And the streaming.example
site is now in a different embedded context. There can be other cases in
which a widget or script needs to write or read a cookie
in an embedded context-- for example, to
validate user identity, check their preferred payment
method, or update settings. Remember, if the widget is
embedded on a different site, then it's hosted by cookies
are treated as third party. When third-party cookies
are not available, they won't be included in
the requests originating from a site different than
the one the user is visiting. So the embedded widget won't
be able to write or read an unpartitioned cookie. Whatever information
the cookie stores, the widget won't be
able to access it. And that's how
some user flows may be disrupted when third-party
cookies aren't available. Here is where Privacy Sandbox
APIs can help you ensure support for those users. Let's walk through some of them. One of the APIs
we have for you is Cookies Having Independent
Partition State, or CHIPS. But before we begin,
let me introduce the concept of unpartitioned
and partitioned storage. Unpartitioned storage
is the storage as we used to know
it historically. It lacks isolation. If a site writes data into
unpartitioned storage,
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
But before we begin,
let me introduce the concept of unpartitioned
and partitioned storage. Unpartitioned storage
is the storage as we used to know
it historically. It lacks isolation. If a site writes data into
unpartitioned storage, it can be accessed by
the site in any context, regardless of
whether the data was set in a top-level
or embedded context. For example, both the widget
embedded on site.example and another-site.example would
be able to access the same unpartitioned storage. If third-party
cookies are available, partitioned storage is isolated. The data in partitioned storage
is associated with the exact context it was set in and cannot
be accessed within a different context. With this knowledge, we are now
equipped to understand CHIPS. Let's take a look. If you don't need to share
the data beyond the pair provider.example and
the site.example, add the partitioned
attribute to your cookies. And keep them isolated. Partitioned cookies
introduced with CHIPS are double keyed by the widget
provider and the embedding site. These CHIPS live in
partition storage with a separate cookie
jar per top-level site. The cookie jar will
only be accessible from the same context,
the widget and the site embedding it. It won't be accessible
by provider.example when it is opened
as a top-level site or provider.example
when it is embedded on another site.example. Partitioned cookies
are useful when the data doesn't need to be
shared outside of the widget embedded context-- for example, for chat
widgets or payment dashboard
site-specific settings. CHIPS is supported by
most major browsers. And other browsers are dedicated
to shipping CHIPS as well. And they are currently
working on it. If you do need to share
cookies between sites, there are other options. Let's take a look at
related website sets. For example, if you own
multiple related sites, you can declare
relationships between them using Related Websites Sets. Within a set, browsers allow
limited third-party cookie access. Related Websites Sets
are useful if you
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
For example, if you own
multiple related sites, you can declare
relationships between them using Related Websites Sets. Within a set, browsers allow
limited third-party cookie access. Related Websites Sets
are useful if you own multiple sites that rely
on a service that you also own. It can be, for example, an
in-house identity service or perhaps something
like a payment link service that resides
on a different domain from the main solution. You can declare
a related website set by creating a pull request
to the Related Websites Sets JSON file in GitHub, where the
canonical Related Websites Set list is hosted. Related Websites Sets are
currently supported only by Chromium-based browsers. If you distribute a solution
that other sites rely on, there are other
APIs that can help. Let's take a look at
the Storage Access API. When third-party
cookies are blocked, your embedded
widget doesn't have access to its own unpartitioned
storage by default. One way to fix this
is to prompt the user to grant storage access. You can do so by calling
the Storage Access API's requestStorageAccess method. With the Storage
Access API, the widget can prompt the user to
grant storage access. With user permission, the widget
can access unpartitioned storage and will load as expected. The Storage Access API can
be useful in many cases where the user has previously
visited the widget provider as a top-level context. For example, if the
user has previously logged in with your
identity provider example and the
identity provider example widget needs
to verify user identity when embedded on
another site or the user has set their preferred payment
method with your payments provider.example. Then they want to pay with
their preferred payment method on another site that
embeds payments provider.example widget. Storage Access API works across
devices and latest browser versions. Another API that can support the
functionality of your solution without third-party cookies is
Federated Credential Management, or FedCM.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
Storage Access API works across
devices and latest browser versions. Another API that can support the
functionality of your solution without third-party cookies is
Federated Credential Management, or FedCM. With FedCM, users can sign in on
sites using account information and credentials from
identity providers, companies that have deep expertise
in user identity. FedCM lets you customize the
user identity experience. For example, you can choose
between different UI modes or configure the
user prompt to state exactly what user
data is requested. Additionally, an
active FedCM session serves as a trust signal
for the Storage Access API. That means that when
storage access is requested, your widget will
automatically get access to its top-level
unpartitioned storage. This accounts for a more
seamless user experience. No additional user
prompt is required. FedCM is a great option for
identity payments and content providers. FedCM is not
baseline, because it's a new feature that is
not yet supported by some of the widely used browsers. However, other
browsers are currently actively implementing FedCM. We've covered a lot
of technologies. But now it's time to get
some real-world perspective. Let's ask Dipti, a product
specialist on Google's Privacy Sandbox team, who constantly
connects with industry leaders and knows exactly what
is driving the market. Hey, Dipti, we are eager
to hear your insights. What's going on in the industry? DIPTI KOTHARI: Thanks, Natalia. Happy to be here. Industry leaders in identity,
payments, and embedded widget solutions understand
how important it is to offer both privacy
and a good user experience to their users. Identity, payments, and embeds
may rely on third-party cookies. This is suboptimal
for user privacy and can impair user experience
where third-party cookies are unavailable. The number of sites and services
that use Privacy Sandbox APIs keeps growing and
includes KG Media, netID, ORY, PayPal, Seznam, Shopify,
Times Internet, Tray, and many others.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
and can impair user experience
where third-party cookies are unavailable. The number of sites and services
that use Privacy Sandbox APIs keeps growing and
includes KG Media, netID, ORY, PayPal, Seznam, Shopify,
Times Internet, Tray, and many others. These companies are
reporting positive outcomes from their adoption. Let's explore how
specific industry leaders are leveraging Privacy
Sandbox to achieve their goals. KG Media, one of Indonesia's
largest media groups, with publisher networks like
Kompas reaching over 70 million monthly active users. Recognizing the growing
importance of user data privacy, they integrated
Related Websites Sets and updated their Google
Identity Services implementation to support FedCM. Their integration of
Related Websites Sets ensures consistent,
personalized user experience within KG Media's related sites. Complementing this,
their adoption of FedCM for Google
Identity Services provides a more
privacy-respecting way for user authentication. This forward-thinking
approach not only maintains a seamless
user journey but also reinforces trust
with their vast audience. Next, I'd like to
talk about Seznam, a leading European technology
company, publisher, and identity provider
reaching over 90% of the Czech population. They have integrated
FedCM to allow users of the e-shops operating
on their partner's platform to sign in using
their Seznam account. In addition, to an
improved user experience and a consistent identity flow. Regardless of third-party
cookie availability, they achieved a noticeable
increase in user sign-in rates on their advertiser network. This demonstrates how
privacy-respecting solutions can positively impact
user engagement. Here's another example of
Tray, an e-commerce CMS provider from Brazil. They adopted a dual strategy. They transitioned
third-party plugins to CHIPS to allow them to
function properly, while limiting the potential
for cross-site tracking, and updated their internal
applications to all be first party to each other.
speaker: unknown

title: Google I/O 2025 – 10oHjdwUIxo
text:
They transitioned
third-party plugins to CHIPS to allow them to
function properly, while limiting the potential
for cross-site tracking, and updated their internal
applications to all be first party to each other. Tray prototyped and validated a
solution with CHIPS in a 15-day sprint with two developers. With this
implementation, they are ready to transition
to a durable solution and provide support for
more users across browsers. These examples from various
sectors, like media, payments, and e-commerce platforms,
really highlight the momentum we are seeing with
privacy sandbox adoption. This isn't just about
a cookieless future, it's about creating a
better, more trustworthy, online world in the present. NATALIA MARKOBORODOVA:
Thank you, Dipti. Whoa, it looks like
companies around the world are preparing for third-party
cookies restrictions. They demonstrate how privacy
and great user experience go hand in hand. Let's sum up what
we've covered so far. We've talked about what
third-party cookies are, which solutions don't rely
on third-party cookies, which identity payments
and widget solutions might be using
third-party cookies, and how to test your site. Most importantly, we've
covered alternative APIs that can help you
support more users. And lastly, we saw how industry
leaders adopt the new approach. Now it's time to test
your site and transition to the new approach. Together, we can build
a thriving internet that safeguards user privacy. Join us on this journey at
privacysandbox.google.com. Check out our identity payments
and embedded widget guides for more tips. If you want to learn more
about each particular API, take a look at the
developer documentation. Thank you for joining
our identity payments and embedded widget session. You might also be interested
in other I/O sessions, like what do cookie changes
mean for web developers? And reshaping user
authentication and identity verification. Check out the links
in the description. And now happy coding. [MUSIC PLAYING]
speaker: unknown
